title: Golden Tempo Wins Kentucky Derby, Making Cherie DeVaux First Female Winning Trainer
summary: In a stunning upset at Churchill Downs, Golden Tempo won the 152nd Kentucky Derby on Saturday, propelling trainer Cherie DeVaux into the history books as the first woman to train a winner of America's most famous horse race. The 23-1 long shot, ridden by Jose Ortiz, was at the back of the pack early but unleashed a powerful stretch run to claim victory in the 1 1/8-mile classic. "I don't even have any words right now," an elated DeVaux said. "Just so, so happy for Golden Tempo. Jose did a wonderful job, a masterful job of getting him there. He was so far out of it." DeVaux, who started her own stable eight years ago, was saddling her first-ever Derby horse. She is just the second female trainer to win any Triple Crown race, following Jena Antonucci's triumph with Arcangelo in the 2023 Belmont Stakes. During Derby week, DeVaux's perspective shifted from downplaying the significance of her gender to embracing her role as a trailblazer. "I'm a role model to girls who might want to follow in my footsteps someday," she acknowledged. She is only the 18th woman to saddle a horse in the Derby's long history. Jockey Jose Ortiz, who also won the Kentucky Oaks on Friday aboard a 3-year-old filly, secured his first Derby win in his 11th attempt. The Derby field was reduced to 18 horses after Great White was scratched for flipping and throwing his jockey; track veterinarians made the call. Earlier, Silent Tactic was ruled out due to a foot injury. The victory marks a milestone for women in a sport long dominated by men, and DeVaux's achievement is already inspiring a new generation of young fans who see that the winner's circle has no gender.
why_it_matters: DeVaux's win is about more than a horse race: it cracks open a door that was closed for over a century. When a barrier-breaker succeeds in a high-profile event, it changes how young people imagine their own futures—in sports, science, or any field where they have been told 'you don't belong.'

keywords.explanation: A competitor, especially in horse racing, that is given very low odds of winning because it is not expected to do well.
The final straight section of a racetrack where horses accelerate and try to pass each other to win.
The set of three major Thoroughbred horse races in the United States: the Kentucky Derby, the Preakness Stakes, and the Belmont Stakes.
A young female horse, usually under four years old, used for racing.
When a horse is removed from a race just before it starts, often because a veterinarian decides it is not fit to run.
A person who is the first to do something significant, making it easier for others to follow in that field.

questions.question: What were Golden Tempo's odds before the race?
Who rode Golden Tempo to victory in the Kentucky Derby?
According to the article, how many women had previously won ANY Triple Crown race as a trainer?
What victory did Jose Ortiz secure the day before the Derby?
Why was Great White removed from the Derby field?
options: He flipped and threw his jockey, and track veterinarians scratched him.
His trainer decided not to run him.
He tested positive for banned substances.
questions.correct_answer: He flipped and threw his jockey, and track veterinarians scratched him.

questions.question: What milestone does Cherie DeVaux's win represent?
options: She is the first woman to ride a Derby winner.
She is the first female jockey to win the Triple Crown.
She is the first woman to train a Kentucky Derby winner.
She is the first owner to win the Derby with a filly.
questions.correct_answer: She is the first woman to train a Kentucky Derby winner.

background_read: The Kentucky Derby (held at Churchill Downs in Louisville, Kentucky, since 1875) is the first leg of the American Triple Crown, a series of three races for three-year-old Thoroughbreds. For most of its history, the sport has been dominated by male trainers and jockeys.

Jena Antonucci made headlines in 2023 when she trained Arcangelo to win the Belmont Stakes, becoming the first female trainer to capture any Triple Crown race. Before that, only a handful of women had even saddled a horse in any of the three races.

Cherie DeVaux launched her own training stable in 2017 and had never before had a horse in the Derby. Her journey from working for other top trainers to owning her own barn reflects the slow but growing presence of women in horse racing's top ranks.

The Kentucky Oaks, which Jose Ortiz won the day before on a three-year-old filly, is the premier race for fillies and is run at the same track on the eve of the Derby. Winning both the Oaks and the Derby in the same weekend is a rare feat for a jockey.
Article_Structure: LEAD: 23-1 long shot Golden Tempo wins the 152nd Kentucky Derby in a stunning upset
└─ HOOK: Cherie DeVaux becomes the first female winning trainer of the race
RACE DETAILS: Jockey Jose Ortiz kept the horse at the back early, then unleashed a powerful stretch run
└─ QUOTE: DeVaux says Ortiz did a masterful job
HISTORICAL SIGNIFICANCE: DeVaux is only the 18th woman to saddle a Derby horse
└─ CONTEXT: She is the second female trainer to win any Triple Crown race, after Jena Antonucci's 2023 Belmont win
PERSONAL SHIFT: DeVaux moved from downplaying gender to embracing a trailblazer role
└─ QUOTE: "I'm a role model to girls who might want to follow in my footsteps someday"
JOCKEY'S WEEKEND: Ortiz also won the Kentucky Oaks on Friday
└─ FIRST: This was his first Derby win in 11 attempts
FIELD CHANGES: Great White scratched after flipping and throwing his jockey; Silent Tactic out with foot injury
└─ AUTHORITY: Track veterinarians made the call
CLOSING MOMENT: The victory marks a milestone for women in a male-dominated sport

perspectives.description: The victory is a landmark for women in horse racing, proving that talent and hard work can overcome historical exclusion. It inspires a new generation of girls to pursue careers as trainers or in other roles where women have been underrepresented.

Some in the industry note that the attention on DeVaux's gender, while warranted, also underscores how much further the sport must go—only 18 women have ever saddled a Derby horse out of thousands of entries over 152 years. The moment is joyful, but the systemic obstacles that kept women out for so long remain a concern.

From a pure racing standpoint, a 23-1 long shot winning highlights the unpredictability of the Derby, where favorites often fail. The focus on the trainer's background can distract from the athletic achievement of the horse and the strategic decisions that won the race.

DeVaux's success may accelerate the entry of more women into top training roles. If she continues to produce winners, it could also encourage more owners to trust female trainers with expensive and promising horses, slowly reshaping the economics of the sport.
